coredata.dc:description: This review aims to explore the state-of-the-art advancements and recent progress in synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) for their application in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) and photodetectors. The key attributes of CQDs with a graphitic core and a surface involving functional groups are solubility in water, non-toxicity, and luminescence tenability, making those materials suitable for many applications. In particular, NCQDs have been recognized by researchers and received much attention due to their excellent fluorescent properties, chemical stability, and green synthesis approaches. The integration of NCQDs into DSSCs proved to boost the performance and stability of these applications by increasing photocurrent, inhibiting the recombination process, and offering enhanced charge transport. NCQDs have a tunable and broad emission spectrum and high photo-stability, making them ideal for photodetector applications spanning through visible and near-IR regions. Techniques like hydrothermal, solvothermal, and microwave-assisted synthesis are explained, which call for attaining the required optical as well as electrical properties of NCQDs. The paper explores the challenges and future prospects of NCQDs synthesis and applications, emphasizing its potential significance in renewable energy technologies.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This review aims to explore the state-of-the-art advancements and recent progress in synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) for their application in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) and photodetectors. The key attributes of CQDs with a graphitic core and a surface involving functional groups are solubility in water, non-toxicity, and luminescence tenability, making those materials suitable for many applications. In particular, NCQDs have been recognized by researchers and received much attention due to their excellent fluorescent properties, chemical stability, and green synthesis approaches. The integration of NCQDs into DSSCs proved to boost the performance and stability of these applications by increasing photocurrent, inhibiting the recombination process, and offering enhanced charge transport. NCQDs have a tunable and broad emission spectrum and high photo-stability, making them ideal for photodetector applications spanning through visible and near-IR regions. Techniques like hydrothermal, solvothermal, and microwave-assisted synthesis are explained, which call for attaining the required optical as well as electrical properties of NCQDs. The paper explores the challenges and future prospects of NCQDs synthesis and applications, emphasizing its potential significance in renewable energy technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel type of nanoparticle with unique and remarkable properties. They have a graphitic core surrounded by a functionalized surface with polar groups, allowing them to easily dissolve in water [1–3]. Two main synthetic routes lead to CQDs: one uses smaller molecules aggregating together, called the bottom-up technique, and the other uses larger carbon structures, such as graphite, graphene, or nanotubes, called the top-down approach [4,5]. There is growing interest in developing green, cost-effective synthesis methods for CQDs, often utilizing sustainable biomass resources. As a result, investigations into the synthesis of CQDs from sustainable biomass resources have been reported more often in previous studies, as evidenced by the following studies [6–8]. One of the well-known methods for CQDs synthesis is hydrothermal /solvothermal techniques [9–11]. This method requires an acidic environment, typically caused by mineral acids like HCl. If the catalysts applied are acidic, then hydrothermal can be used in connection with heterogeneous catalysis. CQDs are produced as a final result of a reaction sequence, starting from this, and going through dehydration, condensation, and aggregation stages [12,13].

CQDs have promising properties as an emerging semiconducting QD, including their extinguished optoelectronic properties, photoinduced charge transfer, adjustable fluorescence emission, and up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL), which may be observed in CQDs that have undergone surface functionalization [14,15]. Due to their non-toxicity and their easy production procedure, CQDs have attracted increasing attention. In addition, desired quantum yield values may be achieved by adjusting them [16]. An important relationship between the size of a CQD and its photoluminescence is the energy gap that exists between its valence and conduction bands. The disparity between the HOMO and LUMO energies is the same as the energy gap between the highest and lowest occupied molecular orbitals [17,18]. Researchers found that the energy gap shrinks with increasing CD size [19,20].

The study also noted that metal-based photocatalysts would eventually lead to the discharge of metal particles [21]. In addition to mediating electrons, photosensitizing, and acting as a photocatalyst, CQDs also have photoluminescent and electron transfer properties [22,23]. Experiments on CQDs in a light environment have shown their remarkable electron donation and absorption capabilities. This is due to the fact that electron donors and electron acceptors are equally capable of reducing the photoluminescence that CQDs produce. CQDs are a more sustainable option than their metal equivalents. It is possible to improve the photocatalytic and emissive capabilities of pure carbon nanoparticles by surface functionalization, doping, or other means. The use of CQDs as photoreaction catalysts offers several advantages. When compared to more conventional catalysts like TiO2, ZnO, and CdS, CQDs excel in all three areas: toxicity, chemo-stability, and solubility in water [24–26]. To modify the absorbance and up-converted fluorescence characteristics, CQDs may have their surface modified. By making this adjustment, semiconductors may absorb more sunlight, which improves their ability to convert infrared radiation into visible light [15,27]. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) are a subclass where nitrogen atoms are incorporated into the CQDs' carbon structure [28,29]. Nitrogen doping significantly alters their properties by enhancing electronic conductivity, improving quantum yield, and modifying surface chemistry, which improves performance in photocatalysis and energy conversion [30,31]. NCQDs also exhibit better light absorption and charge separation capabilities, making them more efficient in applications like solar cells and supercapacitors [32–34]. In contrast, CQDs without nitrogen doping have lower efficiency in these applications.

The world is facing an urgent energy crisis driven by the over-reliance on fossil fuels, rising carbon emissions, and the detrimental environmental impact of traditional energy sources [35,36]. With global energy consumption projected to increase substantially in the coming decades, there is an immediate need for sustainable energy solutions that can reduce dependence on non-renewable resources [37,38]. Traditional solar cells, while effective, have high production costs, are often rigid, and are less efficient in low-light conditions. This makes them unsuitable for a range of applications where flexibility, low cost, and efficiency under diffuse light are crucial [39–41]. The engagement of NCQDs as a new type of nanostructure is a fascinating area of research [42,43], especially applying it in DSSC and photodetector beneficiary mechanisms. NCQDs are preferred for their superior fluorescence features like high photoluminescence, chemical stability, and green synthetic process. These structures have been established to display great promise concerning increasing the efficiency and stability of DSSCs and photodetectors; thus, they are considered as one of the critical subjects of study in the renewable energy field. Zhang et al. (2024) focused on improved efficiency and stability in DSSCs [44].

This co-sensitization phenomenon increases light capture and accelerates charges formed as a consequence of light absorption, and enhances energy conversion efficiencies. The photovoltaic cell operating efficiency (PCOE) of solar cells is 9% as reported by Zhao et al. (2018) in their work. In fact, for the condition where 10 mM NCQDs and 10 mM N719 dye were used in the working electrode configuration, the efficiency recorded was 9 %, as mentioned in the last row in the above table, which was recorded as 8.9 % for plants without NCQDs [45]. Interestingly, the Poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) blended with NCQDs containing a higher percentage of oxygenated surface groups gives higher short-circuit current density (Jsc) and fill factor (FF) values compared to pristine P3HT. Yang et al. (2020) showed that the regulation of the surface states of NCQDs holds the key to optimizing the DSSCs’ charge extraction and the light absorbance capacity, hence increasing the solar-to-electric power conversion efficiency [46]. NCQDs have also exhibited desirable characteristics towards general energy absorption, which is useful in photodetector fields. Tang et al. (2014) noted that layered N-doped GQDs cover the broadband emission from deep UV to the Ni-IR region, hence fitting in the context of visible photodetectors [47].
Both top-down and bottom-up methods can be used to synthesize NCQDs.

In the top-down approach, the necessary NCQDs are produced by decomposing either bulk carbon molecules subjected to nitrogen-rich reaction conditions or bulk carbon molecules doped with nitrogen [48]. Various top-down cutting methods include arc discharge, pulsed laser ablation, electrochemical, microwave plasma, and hydrothermal.

Pulsed laser ablation is a technique that uses a powerful laser beam to vaporize NCQDs from a graphitic carbon source in a nitrogen environment. This eco-friendly, one-step synthesis may be carried out in an ambient setting without further purification stages [49,50]. An inexpensive method, arc discharge involves electric arcing a graphite electrode in a gas mixture including hydrogen, helium, and nitrogen. According to the researchers, the open circuit voltage is 60 V, and the discharge current is set at 100–150 A [51]. Graphene, Ag/AgCl, and Pt wire are the working, reference, and counter electrodes of an electrochemical cell, respectively, in the electrochemical method. Tetrabutylammonium perchlorate is an electrolyte solution that the electrodes are submerged in The graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen are created by exposing them to a potential of around 3.0 V while scanning at a rate of 0.5 V/s. Electrochemical cutting is also known as electrochemical oxidation but the method works under different principles nonetheless. This method is relatively cheap because it involves only centrifugation, yielding NCQDs of various sizes that require subsequent separation. A reliable and effective technology is used to manufacture the N-doped GQDs (graphene QDs) on a Nickel substrate, which is microwave plasma. A microwave is nothing but the high-speed movement of electromagnetic waves in a progressive phase. This process involves sublimating the C60 powder with nitrogen as a carrier gas [52]. This approach is a fast and environmentally friendly method for producing NCQDs because of the following reasons: Peroxidized N-doped graphene is reduced by the hydrothermal cutting method, in which N-doped graphene carbon is obtained from the hydrothermal cutting of the peroxidized N-doped graphene. It also enhances the overall synthesis procedure for complicated compounds, as it's quite efficient. One such method involves bringing fullerene in contact with H2O2 and NH4OH with heating at 120 °C in a round bottom flask, which can be encouraged due to its being cheaper to implement for mass production of nanocarbon discs [53].

K. Periyarselvam et al. used a 100 mL hydrothermal autoclave to combine 20 mL of drumstick resin that had been filtered recently with 2 mL of urea co-reagent. After three hours, the parts were heated to 200 °C ( Fig. 1
a). Redisposing the solution into 15 mL of water was the last step when the process had cooled to room temperature. To remove any impurities that could not be dissolved, the liquid was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min. The mixture of dichloromethane and the brown NCQD solution was then prepared and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min, and the slurry was filtered to eliminate any non-reacting organic compounds. To further enhance the distribution of particle sizes, the top liquid layer was centrifuged three times at 12,000 rpm for 20 min each. The resulting supernatant has a brownish-yellow color. Column chromatography was used to successfully separate NCQDs with smaller particle sizes. The stationary phase used was silica gel, and the solvent was dichloromethane. For characterization and application purposes, the NCQDs were kept at 4 °C to preserve their stability and necessary characteristics [54]. Cesana et al. conducted the electrochemical synthesis of C-dots(N), carbon quantum dots with a structure that includes a nitrogen heteroatom. A final concentration of 0.16 mol/L was achieved by adding 12 ml of NaOH solution to a 200 ml Becker that already contained 150 ml of oleylamine. A high-powered tip ultrasound was used to sonicate the mixture for around 20 min. Afterwards, a calomel reference electrode ( Fig. 1b ) and two 4 cm2 platinum (Pt) electrodes were placed into the beaker. The spacing between the platinum electrodes was maintained at around 1.5 cm. After that, the platinum electrodes were subjected to a 100 mA current and a constant potential of 6.5 V using the Potentiostat-Galvanostat PGSTAT20/Autolab. Here, Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots were created after 16 h [55].

Mumtaz Ali et al. synthesized NCQDs grown on MWCNTs using a rapid microwave-assisted method. MWCNT powder (1 g) was first functionalized by refluxing in nitric acid (100 ml) for 30 min at 80 °C, then freeze-dried ( Fig. 2
a). The functionalized MWCNTs were dispersed in 10 ml of water by sonication, followed by the dissolution of citric acid (0.3 g) and urea (0.6 g) through magnetic stirring. To test the effects of varying concentrations, citric acid and urea ratios of 0.4 g/0.8 g and 0.2 g/0.4 g were also used. The homogeneously mixed solution underwent microwave treatment at 700 W for 10 min. The treated powder was washed with deionized water, dried in a vacuum oven at 70 °C, and labelled as NCQDs@MWCNT. This material was then coated onto Fluorine-doped Tin oxide (FTO) glass to serve as a counter electrode for DSSC. A paste was prepared using 1 g of carbon material, 10 ml of water, and 0.07 g of CMC, and coated by tape casting/screen printing. The printed binder was partially carbonized to restore conductivity, and a solid-state membrane-type electrolyte and separator were used to prevent peeling or short circuits. The photovoltaic performance of NCQDs@MWCNTs was compared to pristine bare MWCNTs [56].

Fig. 2 b shows the GQDs/N-GQDs dispersion synthesis by Li Shen et al. Laser-Induced Graphene (LIG) was created by irradiating a PI film with a 10.6 μm CO2 laser. A 3.5 W laser power and 20 mm/s scanning speed were used to irradiate the PI film for LIG preparation. Scraping the LIG on the PI film yielded LIG powder. Mixing LIG powder with deionized water and ammonia water yielded a 1 mg ml−1 LIG suspension with 0 %, 6 %, and 12 % ammonia water. These suspension solutions were entirely dispersed after 30 min of ultrasonic treatment. Next, an 8 W focused fiber femtosecond laser (FemtoYL, 1030 nm, 365 fs) was employed to laser ablate the suspension liquid at 100 kHz. The LIG suspension was constantly irradiated, and laser ablation created GQDs. Laser ablation of the LIG in deionized water and ammonia produced a light brown colloidal dispersion [57].

Uneven size distribution, little clumping, and desired surface properties in scalable N- CQDs are challenging to achieve. Due to its success in synthesizing large volumes, simplicity, convenience of use, and cost, the bottom-up technique has recently become fairly popular for making CQDs. Furthermore, it has the potential to use several forerunners in the synthesis of monodisperse N- CQDs s.

Synthesis of NCQDs is often accomplished using bottom-up methods. The strategy of mixing nitrogen and carbon precursors is the basis of its operation. The building blocks might be organic substances or biomass byproducts. Dehydration, carbonization, and polymerization are the steps that combine the precursors. These days, N- CQDs are made using a variety of bottom-up approaches, such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave, ultrasonic, microwave-assisted hydrothermal, ultrasonic-assisted hydrothermal, solution plasma, etc. The traditional process involves heating the crucible with the precursors to a point where they undergo pyrolysis in the furnace, resulting in N- CQDs [58,59]. The breakdown of precursor materials by pyrolysis necessitates conditions of high heat and acidity or alkalinity. One common bottom-up approach to synthesizing NCQDs is the hydrothermal technique. This method requires subjecting water and non-graphitic carbon precursors to high temperatures and pressures inside a steel-encased autoclave chamber lined with Teflon. This approach synthesizes NCQDs with a homogeneous particle size distribution easily and inexpensively. This is because the capability of utilizing different precursors, such as organic chemicals and especially cheap bio-waste, is made possible. Finally, the process is not detrimental to the environment, and it enhances the production many fold. However, based on the research conducted by several researchers in this area, the technology has some drawbacks [60]. These are distinct from hydrothermal methods, where water is the solvent; different solvothermal methods use different solvent media. The formation of small N- CQDs in microwave-assisted synthesis involves irradiating solid organic molecules with microwave radiation leading to carbonization. This green synthesis method helps the researchers as it is an efficient, low-cost method with no solvents and a one-step synthesis method [61,62]. There are some limitations when synthesizing the NCQDs; the size of the NCQDs formed cannot be predicted. This prototype is significant in preparing NCQD at low temperatures using methods such as ultrasonic-assisted synthesis. In elaboration, the process involves exposing the carbon molecules to very high ultrasonic irradiation when they are in equilibrium at the ambient temperature [63]. Recently, some researchers have done enhancements to the ordinary hydrothermal technique by integrating hydrothermal with microwave technology and ultrasonic techniques. In the process of hydrothermal synthesis with rationality of microwaves, the autoclave with the precursors is heated to certain temperatures and then transferred to the microwave reaction system. In case of ultrasonic treatment process, the transducer, which is a component of the ultrasonic equipment, was connected to the autoclave [64]. These hybrid synthetic processes require less energy. Plasma in liquid or solution form is a new option to hydrothermal procedures and high-temperature pyrolysis. To synthesize NCQDS, bipolar-pulsed power is applied between electrodes submerged in the precursor solution, creating a non-equilibrium plasma [65].

To make NCQDs, Magdy et al. dissolved 3 gs of urea in 50 mL of orange juice and cooked the mixture for 10 min at high heat in a home microwave until it was totally blackened ( Fig. 3
a). After that, we centrifuged the mixture at 6000 rpm for 15 min to get rid of any particles that had been suspended after letting it cool. The QD stock solution was prepared by filtering the clear layer and then adding 200 mL of water to get the volume to the desired level. Step one in making the working solution was adding 10 mL of the QD stock solution to a 100 mL volumetric flask and filling it up to the mark with double-distilled water. The solutions were refrigerated until they were needed again [66].

Using ethylenediamine and l-lactic acid as carbon and nitrogen sources, Chang et al. fabricated NCQDs in a one-step hydrothermal process ( Fig. 3 b). A 100 mL three-necked flask was used to combine 30 mL of deionized water with 2 mL of ethylenediamine and 30.0 g of l-lactic acid. The mixture was then heated to 150 °C and allowed to react for 12 h. After that, the reaction mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm after letting it cool naturally. Molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da was used to collect the supernatant and transfer it to a dialysis bag. Then, we dialyzed the mixture for 72 h in the glass flume with deionized water. Finally, solid NCQDs were obtained by freeze-drying the dialyzed supernatant in a vacuum freeze-dryer [67].

One of the main ways to enhance the photoluminescence capabilities of CQDs is by doping them. A rise in energy traps and a consequent increase in total faults are caused by the creation of extra surface defects during the CQD doping technique [68]. That is why the quantum yield of CQDs goes up because non-radiative electron transport becomes less likely. Nitrogen atoms are a common dopant in materials, often pushing the emission of circular dichroism towards longer wavelengths by altering the material's electronic structure, leading to a redshift. Additionally, nitrogen improves the quantum yield of fluorescence by increasing radiative recombination, where absorbed energy is emitted as light rather than lost as heat. This enhances both the optical properties and efficiency of the material. Surface energy states produced by the N atom's introduction cause the observed effect, which is an increase of excited electron radiative recombination and a reduction in non-radiative recombination. By increasing the degree of conjugation, amino groups and other N-related functional groups that donate electrons may make excited states in CQDs more stable [69]. When many heteroatoms are co-doped, the performance of the CQD is substantially improved, much more so than when just one heteroatom is doped. Doping CQDs with S and N elements may improve their luminosity, according to recent studies. When N and S were co-doped onto CQDs, the quantum yield was 73 % higher than when the N and S were used alone [70]. Further, S, N- CQDs had a longer lifetime (12.11 ns) than CQDs (7.45 ns). CQDs formed a new surface state due to N doping, and the density of this state was much enhanced by co-doping with S atoms. It was previously thought that different types of CQDs operated by different mechanisms. While compact discs may generate a spectrum of hues ranging from ultraviolet to near-infrared, the most common of them is blue. The wide size range of CQDs is the initial reason for the broad and symmetrical spectrum of most PL spectra. On top of that, various electronic transition paths provide noticeably varied energy levels. A common feature of photoluminescence spectra is that the emission peak changes as a function of the excitation wavelength. Researchers propose that these properties of CQDs make them a promising medium for multicolour imaging [71–75]. In addition, CQDs have a bandgap that is not zero and may glow when exposed to light. It is possible to alter the band gap by modifying the size and surface chemistry of CQDs. When surface functional groups in CQDs are oxidized, excitons are trapped and the emission is redshifted, causing a Stokes shift. Chemical process modifications, surface state changes, and dopant additions allow for substantial control of multicolour emission [76].

NCQDs synthesized via microwave-assisted pyrolysis show excellent physiochemical and optical properties, such as wide spectral adsorption, high charge carrier extraction, fast charge carrier transportation, and tunable emission. The incorporation of NCQDs in DSSCs significantly improves their performance, achieving a high photoconversion efficiency of 8.75 % and a photocurrent density of 18.13 mA/cm². The presence of NCQDs enhances photo absorbance and reduces recombination between the photoanode and electrolyte, which contributes to the increased efficiency ( Fig. 4
a-d) [77].

Table 2 provides a comparative analysis of various synthesis methods for nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), evaluating cost, scalability, environmental impact, advantages, and limitations. It highlights that hydrothermal and microwave-assisted methods are cost-effective and scalable, while green synthesis techniques ensure low environmental impact. Pulsed laser ablation offers precise control but requires advanced equipment. The choice of method depends on the specific application and production requirements.

Every day, energy consumption throughout the world continues to climb [113,114]. No one can deny that the fossil fuel reserves, which are mostly used to generate energy, are steadily dwindling, calling for the investigation of other energy sources [115]. Better living conditions, less dependence on fossil fuels and other energy sources, and a revitalized economy via the creation of jobs are all possible outcomes of increasing our use of solar energy [116]. Renewable and sustainable energy resources are a reality, and they include things like solar thermal, wind, tidal, gravitational potential, and biomass energy. Solar cells, often called photovoltaic cells, are among the most efficient and promising renewable energy sources currently available. The most promising and cost-effective method for gathering solar energy is the dye sensitized solar cell, which was invented by Professor Michael Gratzel in 1991 [117]. Thin film devices known as DSSCs are a more cost-effective substitute for conventional silicon solar cells. Producing DSSCs is a breeze, and they're both cheap and efficient [118]. Applying dye-sensitized solar cell technology, which utilizes natural dyes as photo sensitizers, is the most efficient way to capture solar energy and transform it into electricity. The combination of a dye with little extinction and a photoanode with a much greater surface area (about 1200 times bigger than a flat electrode) makes a dye-sensitized solar cell a suitable medium for light collection. There is still room for improvement in the red wavelengths, but using a dye with moderate extinction in conjunction with a large surface area photoanode the size of a flat electrode allows for substantial absorption over much of the visible spectrum. A dye-sensitized solar cell's basic building blocks are a photoanode and a mesoporous semiconductor oxide, most often titanium dioxide, which absorbs a monolayer of sensitizer, or dye [119]. A semiconductor in a standard solar cell system can both absorb light and store electric charge. Separate materials are used for light absorption, electron transport, and hole transport in DSSCs. When light hits the sensitized dye, it causes electrons to flow through a porous TiO2 structure. In a liquid redox electrolyte, holes are transported, and charges are separated at the point where the dye enters the conduction band of the solid and the electrons produced by photons meet [120].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure of dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:section.ce:para: An average-sized layer of TiO2 nanoparticles (about 20 nm in diameter) forms the mesoporous film that serves as the working electrode. The film is applied on a glass substrate that has been coated with Fluorine-Doped Tin oxide, and its thickness is about 10 μm. The surface of titanium dioxide may absorb color molecules. In addition to facilitating electron transport, the TiO2 framework acts as an electron acceptor [121].
A purpose-fitting concentrated redox couple and other additives that boost solar cell performance make up this solution. When doing DSC, the iodide/tri-iodide redox pair is often used.

The catalytic characteristics of this electrode make it ideal for use in redox electrolytes, where they facilitate electron transport. Restoring the electrolyte is accomplished using the counter electrode. In order to get electrons from the outside circuit, the oxidized electrolyte moves towards the counter electrode. The reduction process often employs platinum as a catalyst due to its transparency, high exchange current density, and strong catalytic properties. The process of plating the TCO substrate with platinum determines the counter electrode's efficacy [122].
To allow the maximum amount of sunlight to reach the cell's effective area, substrates must be very clear (transparency > 80 %). Efficient use also requires strong electrical conductivity.

Emphasizing charge transmission and minimizing energy loss is crucial for optimizing energy efficiency and minimizing energy waste. These two substrate characteristics dictate the efficacy of DSSCs. The most common types of conductive substrates are fluorine tin oxide and indium tin oxide. Coatings of indium tin oxide and fluorine tin oxide are applied on soda lime glass to create ITO and FTO substrates, respectively. In contrast to FTO films, which exhibit a transmittance of around 75 % in the visible region and a sheet resistance of 8.5/cm2, ITO films have a transmittance of >80 % and a sheet resistance of 18 Ω/cm2 [123]. It is common practice to construct DSSCs from two separate transparent conductive layers. In addition to collecting current, these layers provide a surface onto which the catalyst and semiconductor may be deposited.

ce:section.ce:section-title: An electrode based on nanocrystalline semiconductor film
ce:section.ce:para: For dye-sensitized solar cells, semiconductor oxides including SnO2, Nb2O5, TiO2, ZnO, and ZrO2 are used. Because of its high electron transfer capacity and huge surface area, zinc oxide may transport the sensitizer monolayers to the conducting substrate. Zirconium oxide, which acts as a dielectric between layers, is another possible alternative. Currently, TiO2 is the most used semiconductor because to its low price, wide availability, lack of toxicity, biocompatibility, and many uses in healthcare products and paint production [124]. Metal foil, conducting glass, flexible polymer film, and other glass substrates are coated with titanium dioxide coatings. To help the semiconductor materials settle on the substrate, heat treatment is applied to the produced films at different temperatures after the deposition procedures of semiconductors are carried out [125].

In dye-sensitized solar cells, organic dyes absorb photon energy throughout the visible light spectrum and act as photosensitizers. What makes light harvesting and complete photoelectric conversion so effective are the characteristics of these dyes. An ideal dye-sensitized solar cell sensitizer would have a maximum absorption capacity for light just below 920 nm, which is a threshold wavelength. Additionally, it has to have a quantum yield of 100 % and be able to transport electrons to the conduction band effectively while being firmly adhered to the surface of the semiconductor oxide. Even at ambient temperature, dye is able to absorb light effectively and transmit electrons to the semiconductor's conduction band. A chemical bond is formed between the porous surface and the semiconductor [126]. Ruthenium(II) polypyridyl complexes have a broad absorption range in the visible light region, are very stable, and are utilized as sensitizers in DSSC on a regular basis [127]. Even though they have good photoelectric properties, there are a few drawbacks, such as high cost, scarce ruthenium supply, and biological toxicity. Chlorophyll, coumarin, polyene, merocyanine, indoline, and anthocyanins are just a few of the organic colours that have been studied for their potential to cause skin sensitization. Organic sensitizers that do not include any metals, natural sensitizers, and sensitizers that contain complexes of metals are the three main types of photosensitizers [128].

Restoring the dye's electron supply to the semiconductor's conduction band is what the electrolyte is all about. Dye sensitized solar cells rely on an electrolyte layer to transfer positive charges to the counter electrodes. The properties of the electrolyte have a significant impact on the long-term stability of DSSCs. This means the electrolyte has to have this property [129].
1)
The material's low viscosity and outstanding electrical conductivity make it ideal for fast electron diffusion.
2)
Getting the nanocrystalline semiconductor and counter electrode to make perfect contact with each other at the interface.
3)
It would be incorrect to blame the electrolyte for the dye's degradation and desorption from the oxidized surface.
The electrolyte has to be see-through. By donating an electron to the oxidized dye from the iodide electrolyte, the dye can return to its ground state and absorb light.
A DSSC may make use of electrolytes that fall into one of three types: solid-state, liquid-state, or quasi-solid-state.

Solar cells and panels may be made lighter by switching to plastic substrates from glass and silicon. Because of this, DSSCs may be used in places where other solar cell types would look bad, including historically important locations [48]. Even under low-light conditions, DSSCS can generate electric current because they function well in the infrared part of the spectrum. DSSCs, or DSSCs, are an alternative to traditional silicon solar cells that may work in low-light situations (e.g., indoors or during the morning or evening) without direct sunlight. The DSSCs do not include any harmful compounds in their parts. The materials may be easily separated and reused, which is an advantage of recycling over solar cell panels [130]. Due to their cheap manufacturing cost and lack of complexity, DSSCs are made without a vacuum system. According to the study, it reduces the manufacturing cost of silicon solar cells by a factor of five to ten [131]. A wide variety of colored cells, in addition to clear cells, may be selected using organic dye. One possible use for DSSCs is to improve the visual appeal of windows and sunroofs due to their translucence and variety of colors [132]. Aggregates of microscopic particles of optical conversion materials allow for the fabrication of solar cells as thin, flexible sheets. When it comes to traditional solar cell installations, a compass isn't needed to find the best spot for sunshine intensity and incidence angle.

Some of the performance parameters of dye solar cells include fill factor, maximum voltage, maximum current, open-circuit voltage, and short-circuit current. Each of these factors is distinct from the others.
When no current is passing through a circuit, the voltage across it is called the open-circuit voltage.

When a cell is exposed to light and its circuit is not closed, the potential difference between its two terminals is known as the open-circuit voltage. Dark current level and Fermi level (semiconductor) influence it. When the DSSC is in an open circuit, meaning no current is flowing through it, the computation is executed. The cell's temperature determines the open-circuit voltage. We call the voltage that we measure at the open terminals of DSSC the open-circuit voltage. The open-circuit voltage is given by the following Eq. (1) [133]:

When a lit DSSC is short-circuited, the resulting photocurrent density is measured in milliamperes per square centimeter. Light absorption, light intensity, dye regeneration, and injection efficiency are among the several factors that affect the process. Theoretical values of current density may be determined from the IPCE spectra, and they are strongly related to the incoming photon conversion efficiency. In the event of a short circuit, the current flowing through a DSSC's terminals is known as the short circuit current. Researchers state that as cell temperature rises, short circuit current also increases. According to Lee et al. (2005), the formula for short circuit current is Eq. (2) [133]:

The fill factor is a measure of the DSSC's efficiency that is determined by multiplying the open-circuit voltage and current density by the greatest power output per unit area. A high internal resistance value is one of the factors that could affect the fill factor; this, in turn, reduces overall efficiency and causes the fill factor to be low. By dividing the maximum value of the cell's output by the power of the incoming light, one may get the overall efficiency of a dye solar cell in converting solar energy to electrical energy. To arrive at an estimate, one must take the following readings: photocurrent density at short circuit, open-circuit photovoltage at the open-circuit terminals, incoming light intensity, cell fill factor, and so on. These variables are represented in the equation. The integer 4. In dye-sensitized solar cells, ruthenium-based dyes like N719 (also called black dye) and N3 have found extensive application as synthetic dyes. Evidence suggests that DSSCs based on N719 or N3 have the highest cell efficiency. In order to provide a continuous flow of electrons, an electrolyte is placed between the working and counter electrodes. Because of its appropriate redox potential, the iodide/triiodide redox pair is often used [138]. The redox potentials of other redox pairs that are near the ground state of the dyes have attracted attention. These pairings include Br−/Br3−, SCN−/(SCN)3, and SeCN−/(SeCN)3− [139,140]. A higher output voltage is the consequence of this quality. The regeneration of I − via catalysis is aided by the counter electrode. Because of its exceptional performance, platinum is often used as a counter electrode. Regenerating iodide efficiently speeds up its diffusion, which in turn speeds up the reduction of the oxidized dye.

CQDs have emerged as promising materials for counter electrodes in dye-sensitized solar cells, because of their excellent catalytic properties, stability, and cost-effectiveness. Acting as an efficient alternative to traditional platinum electrodes, CDs enhance the reduction of triiodide to iodide, a critical step in electrolyte regeneration. Hasan et al. demonstrated that heteroatom-doped CDs in combination with multi-walled carbon nanotubes significantly improved the catalytic activity and PCE of DSSCs to 4.28 % by enhancing the surface area and charge transfer capabilities [141].

Zhu et al. (2017) employed CDs in bifacial DSSCs, achieving efficiencies comparable to platinum electrodes but with superior stability, yielding rear efficiencies of 7.01 % [142]. Wang and Lu (2015) synthesized graphene quantum dots with carbon aerogels, achieving comparable catalytic efficiency to platinum while surpassing it in stability over multiple cycles [143]. Such findings indicate CDs' potential to replace platinum in large-scale applications.

In addition to enhancing catalytic properties, CDs improve electron transfer and stability. Rezaei et al. (2019) incorporated CDs with TiO2 in DSSC photoanodes, observing a 2.2-fold increase in PCE due to enhanced connectivity and charge separation efficiency [144]. These results position CDs as eco-friendly and high-performance materials for counter electrodes.

Carbon dots play a transformative role in photoanodes by enhancing light absorption, charge separation, and reducing recombination losses. By extending light absorption into the visible spectrum, CDs improve the overall photovoltaic performance. Zhao et al. reported a PCE of 9.29 % for DSSCs using N-doped CDs co-sensitized with N719 dye, benefiting from enhanced spectral absorption and efficient charge extraction [45].

Additionally, CDs can alter the electronic properties of photoanodes. Etefa et al. demonstrated that CDs co-adsorbed with dyes on NiO-based photoanodes significantly enhanced charge separation and reduced recombination, achieving a PCE of 9.85 % [145]. Similarly, Cl-doped CDs were shown to improve charge carrier separation, leading to increased photocatalytic activity and extending absorption into the visible range [146].

Carbon dot composites also improve structural and optical properties of photoanodes. For example, 3D-ordered macroporous Bi:TiO2 structures modified with CDs exhibited superior charge separation and photocatalytic degradation efficiency under visible light [147]. These examples highlight CDs’ versatility in modifying photoanodes for optimized DSSC performance. Carbon dots are revolutionizing DSSC technology by serving dual roles in counter electrodes and photoanodes. Their ability to enhance catalytic efficiency, charge transfer, and light absorption while reducing recombination makes them invaluable for developing eco-friendly and cost-effective solar cells. As research continues to refine their properties and applications, CDs hold immense promise for advancing photovoltaic technologies.

Table 3 presents the performance characteristics of DSSCs employing carbon dots (CDs) either as sensitizers or co-sensitizers. CDs demonstrate versatility by enhancing the photovoltaic performance through improved light absorption and electron transfer. Studies employing CDs as co-sensitizers frequently show better efficiencies, attributed to their synergistic interaction with traditional dyes or quantum dots.

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots have been identified as very promising materials for DSSCs [153]. The incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the structure of these nanoscale carbon-based materials gives rise to distinctive optical and electrical characteristics, rendering them well-suited for a range of applications, such as photovoltaics [154]. The application of NCQDs in DSSCs presents various possibilities to act in several functions that have numerous benefits. Firstly, they can act as sensitizers like organic dyes in order to capture sunlight and create enthused states whereby the new electrons entering the TiO2 photoanode can be facilitated. N-doping in the CQDs enhances their capability to absorb solar energy in the visible region hence enhances the capabilities of the CQDs to capture solar energy [77]. In addition, NCQDs possibly serve a dual role, acting as cocatalysts in the DSSC system [78]. The nitrogen atoms within the carbons integrate the carbons as active materials for electrochemical reactions; this is because electrochemical processes ensure that the dye is regenerated and the efficiency of charge transfer between the electrode and the electrolyte layer is improved. Higher efficiency, better electron transport, and reduced recombination losses can cause the solar cell performance of the DSSC to be higher [155].

In diffused and low light-intensity conditions, applications of DSSC employ N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs); these were reviewed by Samir et al. from the experimental results, it was evidenced that incorporation of NCQDs enhances the performance of DSSC considerably when incorporated in the circuit. Looking at the construction of the real device, the option of four outlined cells had an active area of 2. 5 cm2 and the proportion of N-doped QDs has been fixed to be 3 %. Fig. 7(a) shows the results of the first measurements of the I − V characteristics taken under the one-sun situation, with the AM1.5 G injected by the customized LED solar simulator. The I − V characteristics for the four cells under diffused- and low-light intensity conditions are shown in Fig. 7(b) and (c), respectively. To measure the cell's efficiency in these settings, we devise two novel figures of merit, D45o and L0.2. An intriguing tendency for DSSCs operating under indirect or low-intensity photons is shown in the findings. Under diffused light, the three QD topologies contributed about similarly, with basic configuration around 1.47 and DD45oo approximately 1.55. The inherent scattering mechanism in porous TiO2 is to blame for this restriction. Put simply, the scattering mechanism that lengthens the optical path length within the active layer is the primary component that impacts high angle of incidence diffused light harvesting. When it comes to diffused light applications, mesoporous TiO2 has previously shown an exceptionally high forward scattering mechanism in DSSCs. The scattering medium gains more scattering centers with the addition of QDs [153].

Ghadari et al. showed that nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots may greatly increase phthalocyanine–sensitized solar cell performance. Two dye methods are available to study NCDs: 1) Mixing related Pcs and NCDs produced phthalocyanine-NCD conjugate complexes. Results indicated that NCDs boost Pc-SSC efficiency; 2) Phthalocyanine-doped NCDs were synthesized in situ with related Pcs. Cell parameters indicated that doping NCDs with Pcs significantly improves solar cell efficiency. Phthalocyanine-doped NCDs have differing conversion efficiency values than their conjugates. Total conversion efficiency improvement depends on JSC and VOC. A negative change in JSC values may explain why phthalocyanine-NCD conjugates sensitive solar cells have poorer conversion efficiency than doped NCDs. The JSC values of solar cells sensitized by phthalocyanine-doped NCDs may rise because to the larger dispersion of phthalocyanine in the NCDs than in situ synthesis. Thus, phthalocyanine-doped NCDs co-sensitize dyes better than conjugates. The findings showed that NCDs may co-sensitize phthalocyanine dyes and boost DSSC efficiency. Phthalocyanines like H2Pc, MnPc, FePc, CoPc, NiPc, CuPc, and ZnPc are poorly soluble in water, which is the main drawback of the recommended process for generating conjugate systems and doped NCDs. [156].

Hydrothermal synthesis produced N-GQD@MoS2@rGO and additional samples. MoS2@rGO and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO coated rGO with sphere-like MoS2 nanosheets without aggregation. Cycle voltammetry (CV) was used to study the counter electrode's catalytic and cycling stability ( Fig. 8 a). Fig. 8b displays CV data for Pt, MoS2, N-GQD@MoS2, MoS2@rGO, and N-GQD@MoS2@rGO at 50 mV/s −1. Due to graphene's strong electrical conductivity and MoS2's electrochemical activity, N-GQD@MoS2@rGO outperforms other Pt in catalysis. Fig. 8c shows the CV profile of the MoS2 electrode at sweep speeds from 10 to 80 mV/s at −0.2 to 0.5 V. High synergistic interaction in MoS2 results in two significant redox peaks at −0.1 to 0.37 V (scan rate ∼ 10 mV/s). A scan rate increases from 10 to 80 mV/s resulted in redox peaks moving towards positive and negative potentials while maintaining their original CV curve morphologies without distortion. The photovoltaic property was explored utilizing MoS2, N-GQD@MoS2, MoS2@rGO, N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, and Pt as CE, using N719 dye and commercial TiO2 (P25) photoanode. Fig. 8d shows the J-V measurement profile. Different CEs' photovoltaic and redox current densities are shown in Fig. 8e. N-GQD@MoS2@rGO nanocomposites CEs had a high current density compared to other MoS2 CEs, indicating that carbon-based materials boosted MoS2's electrical conductivity and electrochemical active site. Due to its excellent catalytic efficiency, N-GQD@MoS2@rGO had the lowest Epp value of all CEs and 4.65 % power conversion efficiency. The findings showed that N-GQD@MoS2@rGO composite might replace Pt in DSSCs [157]. NCQDs have emerged as superior materials for DSSCs due to their unique optical and electronic properties, as well as their versatility in various roles within the DSSC architecture. One of the key advantages of NCQDs is their enhanced ability to absorb light in the visible spectrum, a feature attributed to nitrogen doping, which introduces additional energy states and improves the photonic capture efficiency. This characteristic makes NCQDs more effective than traditional sensitizers like phthalocyanines (Pcs). While Pcs are known for their light-harvesting capabilities, they suffer from poor solubility and lower short-circuit current density (JSC) when used alone, limiting their effectiveness in DSSCs [158].

In addition to their role as sensitizers, NCQDs function as cocatalysts, significantly improving electron transport and reducing recombination losses within the solar cell. This dual functionality is vital for enhancing the photoconversion efficiency (PCE), which can reach up to 10 % in NCQD-based DSSCs. This performance is further supported by their exceptional catalytic properties, as demonstrated in configurations such as N-GQD@MoS2@rGO, where synergistic interactions between MoS2 and N-GQDs yielded a competitive PCE of 4.65 % [157]. However, NCQDs surpass even these advanced configurations due to their superior stability, scalability, and cost-effectiveness.

Stability and durability are crucial parameters for DSSC applications, and NCQDs excel in these aspects. They exhibit high resilience under UV exposure and in aqueous environments, making them ideal for long-term solar cell operation. Metal-based catalysts such as platinum, while highly efficient, are limited by their high cost and susceptibility to corrosion, reducing their practicality for widespread use [153]. In contrast, NCQDs are non-toxic, derived from abundant precursors, and synthesized through environmentally friendly methods such as hydrothermal and microwave-assisted processes, ensuring a low environmental impact [159]. These green synthesis techniques further solidify NCQDs' position as a sustainable choice for DSSC applications.

Another remarkable feature of NCQDs is their performance under diffused and low-light conditions. These conditions are critical for indoor and low-intensity solar applications, where NCQDs outperform other materials due to enhanced scattering mechanisms in mesoporous TiO2 layers. The integration of NCQDs introduces more scattering centers within the active layer, effectively extending the optical path length and improving light absorption efficiency [160]. Although mesoporous TiO2 itself provides strong scattering properties, the addition of NCQDs ensures better electron transport and charge recombination suppression, critical factors for achieving higher overall efficiency. Overall, NCQDs represent a transformative advancement in DSSC technology. Their ability to combine high efficiency, durability, and environmental sustainability makes them a leading material for next-generation photovoltaics. Their versatility in functioning under varied light conditions and compatibility with scalable, cost-effective manufacturing processes underscores their potential to drive the future of solar energy applications. Table 4 shows the advancement in NCQDs for DSSCs.

Photoresponsivity is one of the performance indicators used to measure how well photodetectors work. You may think of the light-to-current ratio as the ratio of the power of the incident light on the active surface to the current that light produces. The word "characterized" here means unique traits or characteristics of an object or phenomenon. In particular, Iph stands for the photocurrent and Pin for the power of the incoming light. (ii) The external quantum efficiency: This metric quantifies the amount of charge carriers collected by a device in relation to the quantity of incoming photons with a certain energy level. (iii) This is the quantum yield for the creation of electron-hole pairs or charge carriers in the device's active layer(s) or layer system and is also referred to as the internal quantum efficiency [176]. (iv) Noise Equivalent Power, or NEP is the power at which a system is capable of detecting signals, which is the lowest detectable power level. This is done by the strength of the signal light that gets into the photodetector, providing the signal to noise ratio of 1 within the bandwidth of 1 Hz. Nipper is described in photodetectors as the ability of a photodetector to distinguish between two weak signals of light [177]. (v) Detectivity: This key measure is used to compare detectors with different geometries and provides an estimate of performance of a detector. Hence, as per the study, the unit of the measurement is cm/sqrt/hz/w [178,179]. (vi) Hence, the response time of the system is also a consideration for real world applications. This indicates that the gadget is able to find variations in the light intensity and regulate amperage present. The time taken for the modulated excitation light intensity to change so that the photocurrent goes up from 10 % to 90 % is known as response time [180].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical processes typically employed in photodetector
ce:section.ce:para:
Some of the processes that are involved in the process of converting the incoming photons into electrical impulses include Photo-conductance effect, Photo-voltaic effect and the Photo thermoelectric effect. The photoconductive effect refers to the generation of free charge carriers by the absorption of photons, increasing in the electrical conductivity of the semiconductor. A little current flows between the two electrodes when there is no light and the system is biased. Photons with energies higher than the bandgap energy would cause the device to produce electron-hole pairs, which would then be separated by the voltage provided, when it was exposed to light [181]. When free electrons and holes travel in the opposite direction of the electrodes, the current increases. The device's conductivity was improved by photovoltaic power production. The major difference in the transit time of electrons and holes is due to their different mobility, hence it is useful to look at this difference. The photogenerated electron would have a much greater channel traversal speed than the photogenerated holes if the mobility of holes was substantially lower than that of electrons. There would have been a lot of electrons in the photocurrent, leading to a photoconductive gain, before recombination or hole extraction happened. As a result, there will be more than one quantum efficiency [182] Eq. (5).

The photovoltaic effect, which generates photocurrent, is based on the production of electron-hole pairs by use of light. An internal electric field may separate these pairs; this field can originate from an interface, such as a PN junction or a Schottky barrier. As a result, in the absence of light, the detectors display non-linear current-voltage properties. In a PN junction, the source-drain voltage and current follow an exponential relationship. The reverse current is negligible before the connection breaks down [183]. The current-voltage properties of the PN junction under lit and non-illuminated circumstances. In the absence of an external voltage, a broad photocurrent may be produced as the photogenerated electron and hole pairs are separated by the internal electric field. An open-circuit voltage may be generated when carriers of opposite polarity are built up, which keeps the circuit in an open condition. The photogenerated carriers are removed in the opposite direction during illumination and reverse biasing, which increases the magnitude of the reverse current. Photodetectors that work by utilizing the photovoltaic effect are known as PN diodes. Neither a bias voltage nor a reverse bias voltage is usually necessary for their operation. At zero voltage, the photodetector's detectivity is maximized since the dark current is at its lowest. While systems working in the photoconductive mode have a higher photoresponsivity than those without internal amplification, the reverse biasing of the photodiode may decrease the junction capacitance and increase its speed. This method proves the device can detect very small quantities of light with very little power, which has major inherent benefits [184].

When a semiconductor is heated using photothermoelectric light, a temperature gradient is produced. Furthermore, ∆T is the temperature differential between the two transistor ends. The Seebeck effect will cause the temperature differential to become a voltage difference. A linear link between the magnitude of ∆V and the temperature gradient is shown by the Seeback coefficient Eq. (7) [185].
(7)
Δ
V = S .
Δ
T

The concentrated laser beam is so narrow that it can't even touch the measuring photodetector, yet it can still produce a heat gradient. As an alternative, a heat gradient may also be produced by a localized change in absorption inside the photodetector under the illumination of the whole device. Since pure metal has a much lower Seeback coefficient than the semiconductor used in the device, the name Smetal is unnecessary Theta (ψ). If you want to know how much the temperature has changed, you may either use on-chip thermometers or use finite elements to represent it [186]. Finding ∆T allows for a straightforward estimation of the semiconductor material's Seebeck coefficient. If the electrodes are to form Ohmic contacts with the semiconductor and allow current to pass through the device, their material selectivity is a must. Consequently, without an external bias voltage, lighting a single semiconducting channel results in no current flow and very weak temperature gradients [187].

Photodiodes isolate and collect the photogenerated carrier most efficiently by using an internal electrical field that is established within the device's absorbent layer. By combining materials with majority carriers and opposing charges, an internal electric field may be created [188]. A depletion region with an inherent field and a charge gradient is created when these carriers recombine and inter diffuse at the junction. The field is strongly biased towards the efficient one-way transport of photogenerated electrons and holes, which reduces the likelihood of recombination. The diffusion process causes carriers created beyond the depletion zone to approach the electrodes or the depletion area more slowly. Because recombination might take place more slowly in the depletion zone, it is a crucial factor in performance. a photodiode device with a p-n junction. To reduce transit time, photodiodes are often operated with a modest reverse bias to provide a larger depletion area. But, the response frequency could be adversely affected by a bias that is too high. Materials used to construct the junction of the device are a common way to categorize photodiodes. However, the fundamentals of using an internal electric field to separate and collect charges produced by light are the same, and the interaction between the device's thickness and the carrier diffusion length, which is influenced by the carriers' lifetime and mobility, is vital [189].

The photoactive layer in a photoconductor is encased between two Ohmic contacts, the drain and source electrodes, making it an optoelectronic device. The photogeneration process involves exposing photoconductors in the photoactive layer to light, which may cause them to temporarily alter their density, carrier mobility, or both. The dominant carriers' mobility is greater than the minority carriers' mobility in an appropriate area. This ensures that most carriers can go through the photoactive zone faster and leave the rest behind, while the majority passes through. After the bulk of carriers are in the holes, the extra holes provided by the other electrodes maintain the detector's charge neutrality after they're removed. As a result, the detector may acquire it by effectively cycling holes several times over the carrier's lifetime [189,190]. While increasing gain makes detectors more sensitive, it also makes them noisier, which might be a problem for applications that need very delicate detection [191]. Since the length of trapped carriers is also affected by the temporal response, using carrier mobility implies that photoconductors often have slower reaction times than photodiodes. Infrared applications, such as thermal imaging and motion detection, often use photoconductors [192,193].

The majority of phototransistors are photoconductors, and their construction usually involves a dielectric plate separating semiconductors that are strongly doped or metal. By introducing a gate voltage, the gate electrode enables the manipulation of transport. Commonly referred to as opto-FETs, optical FETs function similarly to conventional FETs [194]. The p-type lead chalcogenides quantum dot film and other conductive channels may have their transport tuned by introducing charges via a gate bias, which fills trap states. By introducing a negative bias, this enhances the holes' conductivity.

NCQDs have flut-like spectral emission, which covers from the deep ultraviolet to the near-infrared regions; thus, they can be perfectly applied in optoelectronic devices such as broadband photodetectors. For example, Nitrogen-Graphene Quantum Dots have shown unique broadband emission owing to their layered structure and accessible π-Electrons. This structure enables broadband photodetectors with high responsivity and sensitivity irrespective of the wavelength [197].

The generation of NCQDs can be carried out via green hydrothermal methods, using easily available precursors like waste paper and urea. This green synthesis is further harmonized to reduce the cost of production while at the same time decreasing the effects of pollution. Such synthesized NCQDs reveal strong luminance and high photostability, which play a crucial role in maintaining the operational efficiency of photodetectors. For instance, NCQDs derived from waste paper displayed six times higher photoluminescence intensity compared to pristine CQD, broadening their photo-response and sensitivity control in visible light operations [198,199].

The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses reveal that the zinc nanorods (ZNRs) are arranged in a vertical array and exhibit a high degree of orientation along the c-axis (002). The photoluminescence spectra confirm that UV light is absorbed by NCQDs adsorbed onto the surface of ZNRs, and there is a potential energy transfer to ZnO. This results in an enhanced narrow band-edge emission (379 nm) of ZNRs array films. The UV photodetector, which consists of a film made of ZNRs array decorated with NCQDs, demonstrates a photocurrent density of 504 μA/cm2 at 8 V when exposed to UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm and an intensity of 3 mW/cm2. This results in a higher device responsivity of 142.4 mA/W at 8 V and an external quantum efficiency of 48.4 % at 8 V, compared to the ZNRs array film-based devices (with a responsivity of 106.6 mA/W and an efficiency of 36.2 %) or the ZnO seed layer-based devices (with a responsivity of 9.3 mA/W and an efficiency of 3.1 %) ( Figs. 9a and b ). The findings indicated that the hybrid structure of NCQDs/ZNRs has great potential for producing highly effective UV photodetectors by solution processing [200].

The surface modification of carbon dots may be tuned by sulphur (S-CQD) and nitrogen (NCQD) doping, allowing for the creation of a near-infrared (NIR) photodetector for use with silicon nanowire heterostructures. At several temperatures ranging from 303 K to 250 K, the ideality factor (η), barrier height (Φ), and series resistance (Rs) are assessed to illustrate surface inhomogeneity and possible barriers at the interfaces of two nanostructures. Fig. 10a shows the simple hydrothermal approach used to manufacture sulfur- and nitrogen-doped CQDs, respectively, and Fig. 10b shows the vertically grown silicon nanowire synthesised from an n-Si wafer using the metal-assisted chemical etching (MACE) method. Fig. 10c shows the devices that were made via drop-casting: SiNWs/S1 and SiNWs/S2. In Figs. 10 d-j the vertical structure of the synthesized SiNWs with a length of 2–6 µm and CQDs attached to their top was shown in field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pictures, respectively. The Cheung model, which suggests that the dopants and interface inhomogeneity may be controlled, gives very little variation in CQDs depending on nitrogen and sulphur. The creation of p-n junctions increases the responsivity (622.22 mA/W) and detectivity (2.15 × 1012 Jones), whereas the electron-withdrawing impact of sulfur on the CQD surface produces a more p-type character [201].

Using a two-dimensional (2D) MoS2 ultrathin layer and zero-dimensional (0D) Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), a unique solar cell was created. Combining NCQDs with MoS2 has strong optical and electrical properties. The quantum dimensional heterostructure's current-voltage (I–V) properties were measured in the dark and under solar radiation. Fig. 11a illustrates the device's design with NCQDs/MoS2 as active heterojunction, ITO-coated glass substrate, and Ag as bottom and top contacts. NCQDs/Ag and MoS2/ITO have virtually ohmic top and bottom contacts [45]. The NCQDs/MoS2 interface significantly caused the current rectification characteristics. Dark current-voltage (I–V) characteristics of NCQDs/MoS2 (0D/2D) quantum dimensional heterostructure are shown in Fig. 11b. Current rectification in the positive voltage region shows that the NCQDs/MoS2 interface needs a p-n junction or Schottky junction. While bare MoS2 manufactured on ITO substrate has almost linear I–V characteristics with a minor rectification. For the ideality factor (n) and current rectification ratio, the dark I–V characteristics were replotted on the semilogarithmic scale (Fig. 11c). The curve's ideality factor was computed for three voltage regions: 1.27 for starting voltage (V < 0.1 V), 6.25 for medium voltage (0.1 V < V < 0.36 V), and 13.49 for higher voltages. The ideality factor for low voltage areas (V < 0.36 V) is caused by band-to-band recombination in the junction. For higher voltages (V > 0.36 V), the computed ideality factor represents the quantum dimensional heterostructure's series resistance owing to carrier entrapment in lattice defects caused by mismatch, impurities, and vacancies. Using the dark ln I–V characteristic, current rectification was computed as 10 at ±1.5 V. Fig. 11d compares I–V characteristics in the dark and under AM 1.5 G solar illuminations at 100 mW/cm2. It shows a considerable I–V curve change on solar radiation illuminations. Solar cell parameters were calculated by plotting solar I–V characteristics in terms of short-circuit current (Isc) and open-circuit voltage (Voc). In Fig. 11e, the I–V curve is replotted under sun light from 0 to Voc. The 0D/2D quantum dimensional heterostructure has 13.49 mA Isc and 0.83 V Voc [202].

The use of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) in and photodetectors has a solid foundation on which further developments can be built upon. To unleash the maximum potential of NCQDs, the following scientific directions should be pursued, each research direction has to be grounded on sound scientific research and discovery.
1.
The creation of new synthesis approaches for the preparation of NCQDs with tuning the structure and characteristics is crucial. Also, methodologies like single-step hydrothermal methods, laser ablation, and plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition are promising routes. These methods can be optimized for having a better size distribution, high quantum yield, and more stability. For instance, LAL, which employs laser irradiation to generate NCQDs, exhibits great promise in tailoring the size and surface chemistry of NCQDs to improve their photophysical properties.
2.
It is equally important to understand the processes that lead to NCQDs possessing improved characteristics. In understanding the influence of nitrogen doping on the electronic structure, charge dynamics, and photoluminescence behaviour of NCQDs, the comprehensive experimental tools including XPS, TEM, and TRPL are vital. Exploring the chemical bond formation and geometry between N atoms and the carbon lattice would help to understand how doping improves light absorption and charge separation.
3.
From the data presented here in the review, it is clear that functionalization of NCQDs enhances their performance in various applications. Doping with other heteroatoms like sulfur or phosphor can lead to additional energy levels to boost the photoluminescence quantum yield charge transfer rates. Surface passivation layers can also restrict the non-radiative recombination pathways, which in turn, can enhance the efficacy of NCQDs in DSSCs and photodetectors.
4.
Mingling NCQDs with other nanomaterials leads to formation of composites that exhibit synchronous characteristics. For example, incorporating NCQDs with other materials such as metal oxide, TiO2 or ZnO can improve light capturing as well as charge carrying capacity of DSSCs. Likewise, when combining NCQDs with other 2D materials such as MoS2, a transition metal dichalcogenide, the responsivity of photodetectors can be improved as well as the temporal response. These kinds of multi-layered hybrid structures can be fabricated using the modern approaches to material synthesis including layer by layer assembly and chemical vapor deposition (CVD).
5.
In particular, NCQDs are of special importance for new trends in the development of photovoltaic technologies, including perovskite solar cells and organic PVs. By using NCQDs in the light absorption layers or as interfacial layers, it might enhance the efficiency and durability of these devices. The tunable bandgap of NCQDs can depend on the doping level and surface functionalization, so it can be adjusted to the optimal range for the active layer absorption, which will finally increase the overall PCE.
6.
It is important to gain an understanding of how to engineer these types of devices in the most effective way to enhance their performance. This includes controlling the thickness and structure of NCQD layers, enhancing the interaction between NCQDs and other materials, and enhancing the NCQDs manufacture to get more consistent and manufacturable. Other methods, like the atomic layer deposition (ALD) and molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) can be employed to grow high quality thin films with thin film thickness and chemical concentration.
7.
The long-term stability of NCQD based devices must be investigated to advance their applicability in practical devices. Further studies should include investigations concerning the stability of NCQDs and their behavior when exposed to different conditions like light, high temperatures, and humidity. Addressing the issue of NCQD stability to increase the service life of devices like OLEDs that utilize the particles is possible through encapsulation procedures and surface passivation. Another area that requires further elaboration is the use of robust components in device design, such as those incorporated into layers that shield the device from the environment.
8.
New opportunities for the practical use of NCQDs may be opened by applying this compound not only in DSSCs and photodetectors, but in many other fields of technology. Possible fields of utilization involve imaging and visualization of biological specimens, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), photodetectors, and quantum information processing. Due to the biocompatibility of NCQDs as well as low toxicity they find application in biological systems in drug delivery and fluorescent imaging. Moreover, it is quite possible to use the tunable optical properties of NCQDs for creating highly efficient LED devices as well as new-generation sensor systems.

Therefore, the specific suggestions on the future research directions of NCQDs are as follows: a) Further improvement of synthesis methods; b) Enhanced understanding of the mechanisms of NCQDs; c) Incorporation of other materials; d) Enhancing the efficiency and robustness of the devices; e) Investigating new applications of NCQDs. Further research work on these areas will certainly unveil the better potential of NCQDs in revolutionizing renewable energy and optoelectronic applications.

These NCQDs have recently become promising materials utilized in DSSCs and photodetectors because of their fascinating optoelectric properties. Studied synthesis techniques: Some of the common techniques at this level are hydrothermal, solvothermal, and microwave-assisted processes, which show the synthesis of NCQDs with more desired characteristics. For instance, in microwave-assisted synthesis methodologies, the quantum yields have been commonly observed to be as high as 70 %, along with the high stability of materials synthesized under UV light exposure; hence, these effective synthesis methodologies are well suited for real-life applications.

The findings of the study have shown that when NCQDs are introduced into the DSSCs, a remarkable improvement in the performance of the developed device occurs. These are; achievements in the capability of the NCQDs to increase access to ultraviolet light energy and convert it to visible light in measurements that have boosted the PCE. Notably, some of the research evidenced on PCE of about 10 % of DSSCs with a thin layer of NCQDs which not only improved the light trapping mechanism but also slowed down the rate of recombination and thus enhanced the general charge transportation rate in the system. A modest investigation carried out in the co-sensitization of DSSCs with NCQDs/N719 dye was found to produce 9 % PCE.

When used in the field of photodetectors, NCQDs offer broad spectral emission, high emission intensity, and stability against photobleaching; thus, they can be used in broadband photodetection. An overall enhancement has been recorded in terms of features of the UV photodetectors-based N- CQDs/ZnO nanorod device with photo-responsivity ratios going up to 142 and the response time of the detector has also been enhanced. Now, the ideality factor that has been with the help of the above formula is calculated as 1. 004 mA/W and the one for external quantum efficiency was determined to be 48.4 %. As a result, these NCQDs have contributed to the increase in forward current density due to their improved ability to absorb light and transport charges better. Some of the unique features of NCQDs are not only favorable on the environmental side of things, but also on the cost side. The processes that were used for their synthesis, including modifying waste materials and biomass, all touch on green chemistry principles and are coherent at the same time with low cost, and low environmental impact. Hence, all these methods not only minimize the probabilities of harm to the environment but also provide an economical means of producing large quantities of products. This implies that NCQDs can be used in varied ways since carbon-related materials are available, and non-toxic for use in different applications.

However, there are lingering issues even with these innovations, as follows: However, there are still unavoidable issues that impede the further development of these nanomaterials: The precise control of the size and surface modification of these nanomaterials, their stability in different conditions, and the comprehension of all the physical improvement factors are still actual issues. One example of such a challenge is the situation where one has to increase the production level to meet market demand for special purpose vehicles and equipment and at the same time check on the quality and performance standards. To enhance the general performance and real-world implementation of these composites further, research should focus on the synthesis methods of NCQDs, combining NCQDs with other NCQDs or other types of materials to also improve the understanding of NCQDs’ interaction with other materials.

For example, earlier research work indicated that there is a tremendous regulatory effect on the surface state management of NCQDs that helps in boosting the charge extraction efficiency as well as the light absorption of the DSSCs, thus increasing the SPICE. As pointed out by other researchers, it was found out that the synthesized layered N-doped GQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence over the UV-NIR region, which perfectly complements the reported visible photodetectors having responsivity of four times. In the case of the proposed module, the Tco will be 31A/W (325 nm) and 56 A/W (532 nm). This paper discusses the findings of the experimental study on femtosecond nonlinear optical properties of single ZnO nanowires by using the Z-scan technique at 532 nm wavelength. High values of fly-back frequency mean that the sample has good nonlinear absorbing properties under the condition of resonant excitation. Nanowire obtained a positive nonlinear absorption value, representing the two photon absorption processes. Thus, the advancement of NCQDs has marked a shift and a new chapter in renewable energy and optoelectronics technologies. NCQDs can be used in optical and electronic devices due to their properties such as high surface area and individual energy level thus the green synthesis from the current work positions NCQDs for future discoveries. It is therefore important that future investigations into the fabrication techniques, the controlling reaction processes, and the device fabrication processes of NCQDs capture distinctive performance frontiers to enhance technological developments in photovoltaics and photo captures.
